title: axe-core Browser Scans, Interaction States, and Manual Accessibility Evidence · Compiler Case: Axe Rule Scope
summary: One axe scan at page load becomes a conformance certificate; hidden menus, errors, dialogs, narrow layouts, keyboard paths, and false positives are ignored. Predict authorization, URL identity, runtime admission, Promise ownership, resource bounds, accessibility impact, failure, and evidence before reading the governing source. Planned artifact: Add and verify the crawler ts axe rule scope increment in stateful automated-plus-manual accessibility review. Learners progress through prediction, ex…
objectives: Run axe-core with declared version, rules, tags, inclusions, exclusions, page state, and complete result evidence.

Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Run axe-core with declared version, rules, tags, inclusions, exclusions, page state, and complete result evidence.

hints: For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend the Axe Rule Scope model · Theory Axe Rule Scope, name the owner, authorization, source and emitted revision, Node, TypeScript, URL, robots, unknown value, Fetch, Promise, AbortSignal, browser, accessibility, report, handle, recovery, and native-transfer boundary, Axe Rule Scope precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend the Axe Rule Scope model · Theory Axe Rule Scope, trace this concrete input and predict values, errors, blocking, and output before reviewing it: A theory TypeScript team handles case 7468 during an authorized municipal service audit. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove versioned axe scans across interaction states plus triage, manual coverage, and regression evidence; browser TypeScript remains deterministic and free of Node, network, filesystem, browser, and host effects; then reorder one frontier, p-limit, Promise, AbortSignal, stream, retry, checkpoint, or shutdown event and prove ownership. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Run axe-core with declared version, rules, tags, inclusions, exclusions, page state, and complete result evidence.

For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend the Axe Rule Scope model · Theory Axe Rule Scope, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: axe defaults and one URL represent the complete site.

checks.pattern: // Evidence: crawler-ts-axe-rule-scope-1lfl7g(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?function\s+evidence_crawler_ts_axe_rule_scope_1lfl7g\s*\([^)]*\)\s*:\s*Readonly<\{[^}]+\}>\s*\{(?=(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?pageStates < 12)(?=(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?reviewedStates < 12 \|\| reviewedStates > pageStates)(?=(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?manualJourneys < 6)(?=(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?suppressionGaps > 0)(?=(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?return)(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?return
